title: Dog Bleeding From Nose (Epistaxis): Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's nose bleeding? Learn why epistaxis is a high-priority emergency and how to document it for your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Epistaxis (a bloody nose) in dogs can range from minor trauma to life-threatening conditions like poisoning; seek immediate veterinary care if the bleeding is heavy, lasts over five minutes, or is accompanied by pale gums.

What is epistaxis and what are the symptoms of a bloody nose in dogs?

Epistaxis is the clinical term for bleeding from the nose. In dogs, this can appear as a slow drip, a steady stream, or even blood being sprayed when the dog sneezes. It may occur in one nostril (unilateral) or both (bilateral) and can be caused by various factors ranging from local trauma to systemic clotting issues.

When is my dog’s nosebleed considered a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Any instance of a bloody nose in a dog should be treated with significant concern. While a minor scrape might cause a small amount of blood, internal causes like rodenticide (rat poison) ingestion, blood clotting disorders, or severe infections are life-threatening. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if:

  • The bleeding is heavy or continuous.
  • The bleeding does not stop within five minutes.
  • Your dog appears weak or lethargic.
  • Your dog has pale gums.

Why should I take a photo or video of the bleeding to help the vet?

Taking a clear photo or video of the bleeding before you head to the clinic can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A visual record allows the vet to assess key clues while your pet is being stabilized, such as:

  • The volume of blood lost.
  • The specific location of the flow.
  • The color of the blood.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs presenting with epistaxis, diagnostic considerations include oronasal neoplasia, dental disease (oronasal fistula), nasal foreign body, and nasopharyngeal disease (foreign body or mass lesions). Initial diagnostics should include a complete blood count, serum chemistry profile, and urinalysis to exclude concurrent systemic disease. If epistaxis is present, a one-stage prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and buccal mucosal bleeding time should be considered in addition to a platelet count. Imaging of the nasal chambers via nasal or dental radiographs is indicated; CT provides excellent images of the nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses. Rhinoscopy and nasal flushing are useful diagnostic tools but should be delayed until after imaging to avoid iatrogenic changes.

Chapter: Otorhinolaryngology, Diagnostic Imaging, Clinical Pathology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1480)

faqs.question: What should I do to stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible to keep their blood pressure low. You can gently apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose if your dog allows it, but do not stick anything inside the nostrils.

faqs.question: Could my dog have eaten something toxic?
faqs.answer: Yes, one common cause of sudden nosebleeds is the ingestion of anticoagulant rat poisons. If there is any chance your dog accessed pesticides, bring the packaging with you to the vet.

faqs.question: What if my dog is also sneezing?
faqs.answer: Sneezing often accompanies epistaxis. It can indicate a foreign object, like a grass awn, stuck in the nasal passage, or an upper respiratory infection that has damaged the nasal lining.
